

### Quarto Document Content


---
title: "Unit 8: Themes, Characters, and the Polish Phase"
format: html
---

## Introduction

Until now, our game has been made of simple geometric shapes. While the mechanics work perfectly, a game's **identity** comes from its visual style and characters. In this chapter, we will swap our colored rectangles for sprites, implement character states with different images, and finalize the "Polish Phase."



---

## Step 13: Importing Game Art

The sprites and art help shape a game's identity. You can find sprites online for public use (like the bus sprite included in your assets folder), or you can create them yourself! 

::: {.callout-important}
### Be Creative!
The important part is to make it yours. Whether you draw a cat riding a rocket skateboard or a simple robot, let the art reflect your vision. It doesn't have to be perfect to be professional.
:::

### Loading Sprites into the Array

For each sprite we add, we must include it in the `load` statement. We then destructure them into variables from the `images` array.

::: {.callout-warning}
### Order Matters
Images are added to the array in the **exact order** they appear in the load statement. Also, always list your image assets **before** your sound files to avoid loading errors.
:::

```javascript
load(
  'assets/background.png', 
  'assets/short.png', 
  'assets/tall.png', 
  'assets/bus.png', 
  'assets/run.png', 
  'assets/jump.png', 
  'assets/slide.png', 
  'assets/jump.wav', 
  'assets/slide.wav', 
  'assets/collide.wav', 
  'assets/lose.wav'
).then(images => {
    // Destructure images into meaningful variable names
    let [backgroundImage, shortImage, tallImage, busImage, runImage, jumpImage, slideImage] = images;
    
    // Your game initialization continues here...
});
```

---

## Step 14: From Colors to Sprites

To display our new art, we need to replace the `color` property in our `Sprite` objects with an `image` property. Additionally, we must provide a custom `draw()` function.

### Why use draw() instead of render()?
The default `render()` function in many libraries displays objects based on the object's logic size. By using `this.context.drawImage`, we ensure the image scales correctly to the `width` and `height` we defined for our hitboxes.

#### Updating the Enemy Logic
Find your `switch(newEnemy)` statement and update the "short" enemy:

```javascript
switch(newEnemy) {
    case "short":
        let short = Sprite({
            x: spawnPos - 35,
            y: -30,
            width: 70,
            height: 30,
            image: shortImage,  // Replace 'color: "red"' with this
            lane: lastSpawn,
            type: 'short',
            active: 1,
            update() { /* existing logic */ },
            draw() {
                // This draws the image to the canvas
                this.context.drawImage(this.image, this.x, this.y, this.width, this.height);
            }
        });
}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
### Task: Complete the Set
Repeat this process for the **Player**, the **Tall** obstacles, and the **Bus** obstacles. Every object needs an `image` property and a `draw()` function.
:::

---

## Step 15: Updating the Game Loop

Now that our objects have a `draw()` method, we need to tell our `GameLoop` to use it instead of the default `render()`.



Update your `render()` function inside the `GameLoop`:

```javascript
render() {
    // ... other renders
    enemies.forEach(e => {
        if (e.type == 'tall') {
            // Special logic for tall obstacles if needed
            e.draw(); 
        } else {
            e.draw();  // Replace e.render();
        }
    });

    if (!player.dead) {
        player.draw();  // Replace player.render();
    }

    tallList.forEach(e => e.draw()); // Replace e.render();
    scoreText.render(); // Text still uses render()!
}
```

---

## Step 16: Character States and Animation

To make the player feel alive, the sprite should change based on what they are doing. We can achieve this by swapping the `this.image` property inside the player's `update()` function.

```javascript
let player = Sprite({
    // ... properties
    update() {
        switch (this.state) {
            case 'run':
                this.image = runImage;  // Switch to running sprite
                if (keyPressed('arrowdown')) { /* ... */ }
                else if (keyPressed('arrowup')) { /* ... */ }
                break;

            case 'slide':
                this.image = slideImage; // Switch to sliding sprite
                if (this.cooldown == 0) { /* ... */ }
                break;

            case 'jump':
                this.image = jumpImage;  // Switch to jumping sprite
                if (this.cooldown == 0) { /* ... */ }
                break; 
        }
    }
});
```

---

## Conclusion

By swapping simple shapes for descriptive sprites, you've given your game a world and a story. Whether your game is about a bus-dodging cat or a sci-fi explorer, the **Polish Phase** is what makes your game memorable to the player.

**End of Unit 8**
```
